title: Boston police seeking suspect in armed robbery
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Boston police are searching for a suspect in a Jan. 22 armed robbery in Dorchester.
The robbery occurred around 4:30 p.m. near Adams and Whitten Streets, involving multiple victims held at gunpoint.
Police urge anyone with information to contact detectives at 617-343-4335 or use the anonymous CrimeStoppers tip line.

title: No license yet for World Cup games as Foxborough asks for public safety funding up front
author: Molly Farrar
bullets: Foxborough may withhold World Cup game licenses at Gillette Stadium without upfront $7.8 million public safety funding.
Boston Soccer 2026 offered assurances via Kraft's financial backing, intending to pay invoices within two days post-game.
Foxborough officials emphasize urgency for timely funding amid uncertain federal security funds for World Cup host cities.

title: Wu to host first-ever ‘office hours’ for residents to speak with her directly
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Mayor Michelle Wu to host first 'office hours' event Thursday at Bruce C. Bolling Building, Roxbury.
Residents can discuss city issues with Wu and representatives from over a dozen city departments.
Event offers city services like census registration and printing vital certificates to attendees.

title: 7 players Bruins could target at 2026 NHL trade deadline
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Boston Bruins may leverage draft picks and prospects to acquire high-upside players before the NHL trade deadline.
Trade targets include Robert Thomas, Conor Garland, Rasmus Ristolainen, Justin Faulk, Simon Nemec, Zach Whitecloud, and Owen Tippett.
Bruins General Manager Don Sweeney aims to secure long-term assets rather than short-term rentals to build a sustainable contender.

title: Staff member at special needs residential program in Franklin accused of breaking autistic student’s arm
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Saturs Allison, 27, from Pawtucket, R.I., is charged with breaking an autistic student's arm in Franklin in October 2025.
Allison allegedly violated Amego, Inc.'s restraint policies during the incident, which was captured on security footage.
If convicted, Allison faces a maximum of five years in prison and up to a $1,000 fine.

title: ‘Every run is about defying cancer’: This 18-year-old is running Boston for her childhood friend
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Amanda Lewis, 18, from Charlotte, N.C., is running the Boston Marathon for the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute.
Lewis lost her childhood friend Lily to brain cancer at 13, inspiring her to run for cancer research.
Running became a coping mechanism for Lewis, teaching her emotional resilience and helping her honor lost friends.

title: Fall River man sentenced for raping woman when she refused to take naked pictures of young daughter
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Elias Rodrigues Dos Santos Mendes, 55, pleaded guilty to rape and child pornography charges in Fall River Superior Court.
Mendes was sentenced to eight to ten years in prison and will be deported to Brazil post-sentence.
The charges followed his assault on a woman after she refused to take nude photos of her daughter for him.

title: What’s next for Patriots’ offense after releasing Stefon Diggs?
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Patriots release Stefon Diggs, clearing nearly $17 million in cap space for 2026 offseason.
New England considers free-agent Alec Pierce or trading for Eagles' A.J. Brown as potential WR1.
Patriots may draft WR at 31st overall if free agents or trade options are not viable.

title: Each mile is for her miracle: This Granby mom is running the Boston Marathon with her daughter in mind
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Brianna Poehler from Granby, Mass., is running the 2026 Boston Marathon for her daughter, a liver transplant survivor.
Brianna supports Boston Children's Hospital through the Miles for Miracles program, honoring the care her daughter received.
She aims to raise funds for research, treatments, and care, reflecting on her daughter's resilience and the hospital's support.

title: Do you support the U.S. strikes on Iran? Here’s what 300 readers said.
author: Autumn Sloboda
bullets: A Boston.com survey had 350 reader responses on U.S. strikes on Iran; 54% supported, 43% opposed, and 3% were unsure.
Supporters argue strikes are necessary due to Iran's support for regional conflicts and believe pressure could weaken the regime.
Opponents fear escalating conflict and criticize the lack of congressional approval, highlighting a need to focus on domestic issues.
